content:
Good afternoon folks, hoping everyone is doing well. This will likely be the last review for this week.
- I have personal commitments with my wife and home, including unpacking.
- My trading room still needs organizing, and a gift needs to be hung on the wall, which I promised to show
once complete. This will probably be done this weekend.

Reviewing the daily chart of the Dollar Index:
- We are looking for lower prices, targeting the sell-side liquidity pool.
- Price has been meandering below the fair value gap, trading up into consequent encroachment.
- There's been a reluctance to go below a specific low.
- I suspect tonight into tomorrow, we will see a large candle reaching below that low, possibly digging into
the 101.00 big figure.
- If this decline is sustained, the 100.00 level could eventually be reached, which would allow for upside in
the Euro, Cable, and other foreign currencies.

Analyzing the Euro Dollar daily chart:
- We have taken out the buy-side as I mentioned the last time we talked about Euro Dollar.
- I mentioned if it kept going, we would be reaching for the buy-side liquidity.
- We're in striking distance of that, and should we get animated to the upside, a SIBI here in the form of a
fair value gap in this down-closed candle would be the next draw on liquidity above this buy-side.
- What would change this?
- If we trade below the mean threshold of this down-closed candle. The midpoint of that candle, draw that
out in time.
- If it trades below that, then I'd probably more likely be reaching for this as a return for an equilibrium type
short-term discount.
- It would be within this breaker (low, high, lower low). We've never really come back to that except for a
small, brief retest (not a retest but a retrade).
- So, it retrades to it and then leaves it.
- If we do break below the midpoint of this down-closed candle, then look for the breaker. But I'm
specifically looking for buy-side, then the SIBI here.

Regarding the Pound Dollar, please remember I am not actively trading Forex, but covering it for those who
are still digging into those Forex pairs:
- We have the remaining balance of this sell-side imbalance, buy-side inefficiency with the buy-side liquidity
here, and here, and here.
- So, it's really likely that we're going to punch through that, look for 1.2500 even big figure.
- Anything on the animated upside would be a buy-side liquidity pool resting around 1.2667.
- What changes this? If we take out the low here.
- So, I'm bullish on Pound Dollar unless we take out this low.

Examining the NASDAQ daily chart:
- Take your attention up to this old high here. So, that is a key level at 12339.00 even.
- Then the consequent encroachment of that wick.
- Definition of a Wick as a Gap:
- Some of you are asking what I mean by 'a wick is a gap'.
- The algorithm refers to a wick as a gap.
- The gap will want to most likely be treated to its consequent encroachment, which is the midpoint of the
gap.
- Not to be confused with a mean threshold, which is the middle (or 50%) level of an order block.
- My order block theory and gap theory are distinct; they're very similar in some ways but very different in
others. We'll go over more of it as we go through this year in 2023's mentorship.
- Over here we have two down-closed candles which is the bullish order block.
- If you look at the candle's high here to this candle's low, that's a buy-side imbalance, sell-side inefficiency
fair value gap, but specifically categorized as a Bisi (buy-side imbalance, sell-side inefficiency).
- The market trades down into it here and trades into the order block's open to the high.
- So, both of those levels here, be mindful of this blue shaded area as this imbalance from here to here on
this single candle, and the order block are these two heavy blue lines.
- So, it looks like it traded down there, picked up a discount, pulled off the low, and we'll probably look to
trade up into this level here (12149.00 even) tomorrow going into Friday.

On the NASDAQ hourly chart:
- Right away, I should point to this clean level here (relative equal lows).
- Here's that daily Bisi (buy-side imbalance, sell-side inefficiency) right here, this blue shaded area.
- But it's aimed at this range from here to here; that's what it's showing.
- The order block high and open on the daily chart, that's what these levels are here.
- So, you can see how we ran up, took out the short-term high, broke lower.
- We have a high, low, higher high. This down-closed candle is what? A bearish breaker.
- Price trades below it, comes back up, bumps it, sells off, and sells all the way off to take out the sell-side
liquidity.
- There's an imbalance here and an imbalance in here, and we're going to take a closer look at both of
those.

Analyzing the NASDAQ on the 15-minute time frame:
- You can see this sell-side liquidity pool here. The clean jagged edges are the result. Smooth edges like to
be made, but jagged.
- Fair value gap here. Market trades down into that, comes back up as a support, rallies through the short
- term high, trades up into the imbalance here.
- What I'm watching tonight into tomorrow is, do we continuously press higher? It looks like it's trying to give
like a three-drive pattern (one, two, three). I don't trust that. I really don't trust it.
- I feel like they're going to try to send it higher. I might be wrong, and I'm completely comfortable being
wrong, but this feels like they want to send this thing higher.
- This high, while it is a nice breaker, it actually has multiple breakers in it:
- We have the high, the low, the high, and this down-closed candle. Project it forward; that's the reaction I
showed you before we dropped down from the 60-minute chart to the 15-minute chart here. That was the
reaction we saw dropping.
- But there is another breaker through high, low, higher high, just as well as you can use this here: high,
lower, higher, than this down-closed candle. You project that forward, we traded into that. We went above a
little bit here, although this candle's high right there.
- So, I'm watching to see do we disrespect this SIBI?
- This actually is a balanced price range, and it needs to get through that.
- Should we get through all of this range in here and trade aggressively above that, then the levels I
mentioned on the daily chart are within reason.
- Otherwise, we could chop around. I don't see a sell-off here yet, like I don't expect a big dramatic sell-off
because we had pretty much that, you know, overnight and on the 24th, which I outlined real-time showing
you in the charts (referring to ES, not NASDAQ, but nonetheless it's the same thing).

Continuing with the NASDAQ 15-minute time frame, focusing on imbalances:
- We have sell-side and the fair value gap. It digs down into that.
- It's also the low end of what? The sensitive price point of a bullish order block, which is the opening price
and the high.
- So, it trades down into it, hits it, and then reacts.
- Let's take a closer look inside this area.

section_title: LIQUIDITY AND HIGHER TIME FRAME CONFLUENCE
content:
Dropping down to a two-minute chart for detailed analysis.
- Why two-minute chart? To save time, and this is the chart that has all the details that you would have
worked down through anyway.
- We have buy-side here (smooth relative equal highs). Breaks order block, trades back to it here, sells off.
- Trades into our imbalance, digs into the lower end, which is the open price on the down-closed candle on
the daily chart, which is a low order block.
- This is the high of the daily bullish order block.
- So, go back through this video a couple of times. This level is the opening price on the daily chart down
candle at the beginning of the discussion for NASDAQ.
- We trade down into it, taking out sell-side here, and then trades higher.

Continuing on the NASDAQ 2-minute chart:
- Trades back down into a fair value gap after sell-side has been taken and a higher time frame order block
has been traded to.
- Now, it rallies.
- Trades above short-term high, so now we have a shift in market structure.
- Fair value gap here, trades down into consequent encroachment between the candle's low and the
candle's high here (midpoint is here).
- We have a bullish breaker:
- Low, high, lower low. Take the body of the most uppermost candle, extend it forward.
- You're doing it with the up-closed candle, not the down-closed candle, when you're using bullish
breakers.
- Extend it through; you can see we're finding some support in here (consequent encroachment).
- You want to see gaps like that in this stay open; it would promote the idea that you may be seeing a
breakaway gap.
- This market rallies back up into the last up-closed candle. This would be a breaker by any other definition,
but because we are elongated from that high, in order to keep stretching further when we trade back to it
and above it, it's mitigation.
- Clarifying Mitigation:
- Mitigation is the action of the trader that is offside.
- Order blocks and candles are not mitigating anything. Prices are allowing the trader to mitigate their
offside position, mitigating potential drawdown or removing it altogether.
- So, that was the function of mitigation.
- Trades down into here. Also, we have a buy-side imbalance, sell-side inefficiency. This candle's high, this
candle's low. We dip into that partially. It's supporting.
- Why didn't we come down to this level here? Because the mitigation block leaves some of that open,
expect it to be an institutional order flow entry (partial entry to a fair value gap) before resuming higher.
- The market reaches up into the buy-side liquidity here, creates a bullish order block, trades down into
here, rallies, fair value gap, consolidates, accumulates more long positions, and rallies away.
- What level is this accumulating longs at? At the high of the daily bullish order block wick.
- The wick down here is the daily order block opening price, so this is the high wick area of it.
- Then we have market rally up, order block retest here, and continues to move higher.

Afternoon session on NASDAQ, dropping into still the two-minute chart. New York lunch begins and ends
here:
- Here's that run from the order block and then the fair value gap.
- Rally around the high of that daily bullish order block wick high.
- Rallies order block, as I mentioned before we transitioned into the PM session slide here.
- Institutional order flow integral right there (I don't have that, I apologize).
- Fair value gap with an order block: trades into it here, rallies, order block (cut through candles, folks).
- Little order block rallies, another order block (one, two, three).
- Why do I only use this one candle here, and might I use all of these here? Because all these candles here
are going against order flow. Order flow is bullish.
- So, if there's a series of down-closed candles and we rally through it strongly like we do here, go back to
that original high candle, extend it forward.
- We have an imbalance, yes. We have a potential order block here, but I'm not interested in that. Why?
Because the candle's body on this one goes lower than that one.
- There's rules, there's levels of all this, folks.
- The market drops into the order block, accumulates, rises again. Small little fair value gap in here
(between this candle's high and this candle's low). Extend it through, accumulates longs here, here, and
then once more over here. Why? Because it needs to go higher.
- That last hour of trading, the macro, runs from 3:00 to 4:00.
- Change down to your block, everyone thinks the high is in place, pumps it through, taking out buy-side.

Rolling on over into E-mini S&P, this is our daily chart:
- Obviously this high is important (I just don't have it on the chart because I want you to see the distinctions
between these levels).
- We have the consequent encroachment of this wick: 4117.75.
- Consequent encroachment of this candle's wick, which is 4072.75.
- Much like NASDAQ, we had accumulation from this original fair value gap here, bouncing, trading up to
and stopping here.
- Most traders, like I mentioned, would expect this to be a sell-off. We walked through this on live tweets
and discussions and watching my commentary throughout the day.
- Then the market rallied through, attacking the buy-side here, as I was mentioning we would likely see
(referring to the Twitter storm that I send out in terms of market commentary and things to keep your eye
on and study tape read via my tweets).
- The market does in fact deliver that.
- Then we had this move here. Previous day we had this small little candle, which is what? An inside day.
- Inside day because its high is lower than the previous day and its low is higher than the previous day. So,
we're going to get a large range day whenever we see this type of scenario.
- Now, I like the idea that we came all the way back down into this buy-side imbalance, sell-side inefficiency
(this green candle here).
- The buy-side imbalance, sell-side inefficiency fair value gap is framed with this candle's high and this
candle's low. Split this candle's range between here and here. This is consequent encroachment.
- Look how nicely it's beautiful, it's almost like poetry how it just drops like this, hits the consequent
encroachment (completely algorithmic) and then reprices higher.
- Now, intraday this is going to look like it's 'going to hell'. 'Oh, it's going to crash.' Yeah, it feels like when
you're watching these lower time frames.
- But you have to have a baseline on what the higher time frame daily chart is, because the algorithm is
predominantly using reference points on this time frame here.
- So, narrative is really closely linked to this time frame, so you need to know your key levels off of this daily
chart.

Carrying those ideas and levels over into the hourly chart for ES:
- You can see here, here's that buy-side imbalance, sell-side inefficiency.
- The market rode up on the pass through here, consolidated (relative equal lows), so you know what's
going to be resting below that, right? Sell-side liquidity.
- We had a small little imbalance (that's purple, it's hard to see it, I know) and we have this one here, and
then we have this one on here.
- It's better to leave this one open. Why? (This is the part where you write down in your notebook):
- When you have a larger imbalance like this blue shaded one here from the daily chart, if I'm bullish, I
want to see the majority of the reactions in the upper portion, which would be the premium level of the
imbalance.
- But when you're looking for continuations, don't see this trading down to this level here in the upper
portion, like I have here (the upper half of the fair value gap, that area here, a premium).
- On a higher time frame level, when bullish, when it trades back down into it, it creates what? An
inversion.
- So, premium becomes discount. That part is extremely confusing, and I promise you we will revisit this
throughout the course of 2023.
- Just know that it's better; you want to know the answers to why I know some of these imbalances are
going to stay open, right? Well, I'm teaching it to you, and this is what I meant by 'I just can't do a five
- minute training video', 'I just can't write a paragraph or a tweet and explain it' and you can run away and
start making courses about it because you still don't know what I'm talking about.
- The whole point is there are some imbalances that are going to remain open. Some I want them filled.
The ones in the upper half of this fair value gap on the daily chart above consequent encroachment, I want
to see those filled.
- It's even stronger if they don't fill in, but the ones in the lower half, they are likely to stay open; they're
better to stay open.
- If they come down and fill in, then it's not as bullish, and the algorithm may chop around and consolidate
longer, and then I have to wait for more information.
- But if I see these types of scenarios here, it's almost like a loaded deal, like a lead pipe cinch trade, you
know? It's just easy, like it's a sledgehammer, like it's pretty much next to a sure thing that you're going to
get a type of reaction like this here.
- And that's why sometimes when you see me trade, I can go right in there when it's just driving straight
down like a falling dagger, and I'll catch it right when it's going into the lowest low, and I'll buy it right there,
which seems crazy.
- But it's these types of scenarios that I do that in when I can see what the framework is and trust that you
know these areas down here won't be traded to.

Continuing with the ES hourly chart:
- So, we have an imbalance in here (keep your eye on that pink area there).
- Then we have this wick as consequent encroachment, that's the next objective at 4043.25.
- And the rejection block we saw that traded to today, 4038.75.
- Let's dig into the perspective of this entire range from here to here. Here's equilibrium. So, we are in
what? A discount.
- So, even though we're in the upper portion of that daily fair value gap in the form of buy-side imbalance,
sell-side inefficiency (that's that blue shaded box here), the upper portion of that is the ideal scenario for
what?
- Lower intraday charts trading down into that, creating a discount relative to this low to this high.
- So, you can start to see how this web, it's very, very intricate as we drop from a higher time frame down to
the lower time frame.
- And if you don't know your stuff, or more specifically my stuff, you can get easily inundated and lost.
- Which is why algorithmic theory must be understood. You just can't look at down-closed candles, assume
they're order blocks, and you can't look at just every individual candle that may resemble a fair value gap
and assume that you're going to have a trade on that.
- There has to be narrative wrapped around it. And none of you that are running around trying to teach my
course materials have any idea what you're doing. So, stop. Okay? Just stop. You're hurting other people.
- The whole point is there are some imbalances that are going to remain open. Some I want them filled.
- The ones in the upper half of this fair value gap on the daily chart, above consequent encroachment, I
want to see those filled. It's even stronger if they don't fill in.
- But the ones in the lower half, they are likely to stay open. They're better to stay open. If they come down
and fill in, then it's not as bullish, and the algorithm may chop around and consolidate longer, and then I
have to wait for more information.
- But if I see these types of scenarios here, it's almost like a loaded deal, like a lead pipe cinch trade, you
know? It's just easy, like it's a sledgehammer, like it's pretty much next to a sure thing that you're going to
get a type of reaction like this here.
- And that's why sometimes when you see me trade, I can go right in there when it's just driving straight
down like a falling dagger, and I'll catch it right when it's going into the lowest low, and I'll buy it right there,
which seems crazy.
- But it's these types of scenarios that I do that in when I can see what the framework is and trust that you
know these areas down here won't be traded to.

On the ES 15-minute time frame:
- You can see that we dropped down from the fair value gap over here.
- We went below the low here, went below it, came back up, look how it's really respecting that one more
time here (consequent encroachment, midpoint of the pink shaded area).
- And then it acts as what? Real resistance as it acted support, right?
- If we go through it like we do here, it can come back up and then what? We start to work down. This
whole shaded area becomes what? A balanced price range.
- It's delivered both sides (down, up) and then left it. It's come back in, consequent encroachment, that's
the last line of defense, and goodbye. Nasty plunge, drops lower.
- Creates a small little SIBI here (fair value gap), consequent encroachment, beautiful delivery down into
our lower level hourly fair value gap.
- Digs into it perfectly here (well not perfectly, went over by one quarter of a point, so one tick).
- Then we consolidate here, create a fair value gap, as I mentioned live, one-minute chart, and then rallied
up.
- I was watching all this stuff on my phone, admittedly, I was sneaking on my telephone, walking around
being with my wife today.
- So, it was no harm, no foul, but I was in the stealth mood trying to, you know, give you commentary. I
wasn't supposed to be on Twitter today, but I surreptitiously did it (sneaky ICT).
- Notice also this SIBI, project that through, and you can see we come back down into it in consequent
encroachment once more, bam!
- You like that 'bam'? Oh, you're going to hear all kinds of wild stuff when I'm doing live streams, some of
the things you've never heard me say, and whenever there's animation on my part, and especially if my son
does something right, I'm going to be like cheerleading him. So, yeah, just get yourself ready for all that
business.
- The rallies up, takes out buy-side here, digs into the last up-close, little change in the state of delivery
here.
ES 5
- MINUTE CHART:

section_title: BULLISH SHIFT AND ORDER BLOCK LOGIC
content:
Let's drop into the five-minute chart.
- Okay, here's the five-minute chart.
- You see sell-side liquidity here, digs below it. It's the hourly fair value gap, the one at consequent
encroachment.
- Remember, we don't want to see the lower half of that daily buy-side imbalance, sell-side inefficiency,
which is that big blue shaded area here. We don't want to see it trade down below consequent
encroachment.
- We're focusing on the upper portion of that, because if it's going to do its turn here and start presenting all
these arrays that we can time off a long or a shift higher in price, then it's better for it to do that there than
to dig into the lower portion of that buy-side imbalance, sell-side inefficiency on the daily chart.
- Now, this is one of those videos where you have to watch it a few times because what I'm saying isn't
going to register until you watch the first portion of the video that I've said so far pertaining to ES.
- Because it takes a couple of times to see where we're at in the lower time frames, what these areas are,
and you can quickly see why I am not painting all this stuff on my charts when I'm watching it, because to
me this is extremely busy.
- But I'm showing you the overlap of all of these convergences that the algorithm is going to refer to.
- It's not supply and demand, and it's not support and resistance. It's these ranges the market's going to
key off of: very specific prices (the high, the middle point, or the low).
- And there's logic into knowing which one you want to see hit and which ones you don't want to see hit.
- And I'm going to do that throughout this year, so that way, that's the only way you can learn it. That's the
only way you're going to learn it because knowing what I know, what's been codified, I can't just simply say
these are the rules that are going to work every single time.
- There's going to be things that the algorithm is going to do based on what factors are in price action at
that time, on that day of the week, and that time of day.
- So, all those things coming together and being equal, you're going to have certain rules and processes
that you're going to have to consider.
- I'm not going to teach you every possible scenario because that would take years, many years, and
frankly, I don't promise that to you anyway. That's pretty much what makes me who I am, and my family
members are privy to that, but the general public, like all of you, I don't owe that to you.
- So, I'm doing as much as I can, obviously, and much more than any of you really would have discovered,
so I'm hoping that this is going to be enough this year for somebody (it won't be, and that's okay).
- But I want you to take your attention in here. We have this candle here, this candle here, and then this
candle right there.
- So, the market reacts from the low end of the hourly fair value gap that joins and links with the
consequent encroachment of that daily buy-side imbalance, sell-side inefficiency (the blue rectangle).
- It rallies up, creates a short-term high right there, and then this candle pierces it, creating (when this
candle here closes) here.
- So, the market trades down into it here once more, opens, and then runs away.
- Order block, trades down, retouches it, rallies, consolidates, rises again into our old shaded area.
- Dips back down. Is it going to be resistance here? No. We have now turned the corner down here.
- I mentioned that if we went to 3977.75, that's the end of the line. That means it's over; the bear sentiment
is no longer valid. We would be considering what? The end of their sentiment is now bullish.
- Go watch and look at the tweets (I'm not going to show you in the tweets, that's your job to go through the
tweets).
- And in here, I give a very specific candle to talk about a fair value gap, and we'll look at that when you're
driving on the lower time frames. But it rides away and it comes back down into the buy-side imbalance,
sell-side inefficiency here, what? The order block trades to a bear, rallies through, and presses into the
afternoon 4038.75 level.
ES 1
- MINUTE CHART:

section_title: JUDAS SWING AND MARKET STRUCTURE SHIFT
content:
On the one-minute chart:
- You can see how we dropped from that 15-minute time frame fair value gap. We traded up into it.
- Here is the Judas swing after taking buy-side liquidity. This right here had everybody thinking it's going to
rip higher. I was watching several live streamers this morning; they got really animated here. They were
actually wishing and cheerleading, hoping it goes higher, hoping it goes higher.
- And the more they did that, the more I felt confident that it was going to sell off.
- So, I actually sold off a position here, built it larger here, and rode it all the way down into this level here.
- And then in here, I went short, building up another position, and then I was calling all this stuff on the fly
on a telephone.
- So, I was looking at all the things I look for, doing all the measurements I look for (Fibonacci extensions),
levels, a little higher time frames working down, and I came to the idea that 3962.50 would be likely.
- So, I had a limit order to get out at 3962.75, and it didn't give it to me; the market didn't actually go that
low.
- The market did in fact rally here.
- Look at this high here. It's great with alignment imbalances. See this high here? This candle's high is
higher than that one, and then this candle is lower than that one. This is your swing high.
- The market rallies through that's a shift in market structure.
- 'But ICT, it didn't close above that high!' I didn't say it had to. Go back and watch the videos on the 2022
model.
- A shift in market structure does not ever require a close above a short-term high when it's shifting bullish.
- It does not require a close below the swing low when it's shifting bearish.
- Stop spreading misinformation and stop perpetuating ignorance. This is the reason why people complain
to me, because they're learning from dollar menu mentorships, and they're not learning from someone that
can do it. Number one, they didn't author it. They have no idea what it is. They have no idea when it won't
work, when it's likely to be there, when it's not likely to be there.
- So, that shift here is bullish, so this drop down here, it's a suspect decline. It's a Judas swing in many
ways.
- Where's it going? Where have I got? Trades in consequent encroachment (midpoint). How nice is that?
- The market rallies, takes out the short-term high here. Another fair value gap. Here's where I stated,
'Look, look at that candle right there on a one-minute chart.' That candle is your fair value gap.
- And I said, 'You got homework today. I gave you a task of looking for two; find two five-handle price runs
before noon.'
- Well, I know some of you were not able to get two, so I'll give you one today.
ES 1
- MINUTE CHART:

section_title: HOMEWORK, TRADE CALL, AND AFTERNOON RALLY
content:
On the ES 1-minute chart, reviewing the homework:
- Here is the candle that range extended through. It touches it here, here, here, here.
- And then I said you want to take your attention to 3982.00 buy-side liquidity, which is this short-term high.
Why that one? Because it's going to offer five handles easy.
- And it delivers like gangbusters.
- I'm walking around, I'm looking at my phone, so I'm looking at all this price action here through the lens of
what? My Galaxy Note 10. My cell phone can only show me so much, and my 50-year-old eyes, they're not
as good as they used to be.
- So, it is what it is. It ran off a lot more than I thought, and I was going to be able to catch something in the
afternoon, but it did the majority of the run right after me calling this live on Twitter.
- It just left, you know. Elvis left the building right there, boom! And his stocking stuffer went with him.
- So, we consolidate in here, rip through, took the buy-side here, back into that imbalance on the hourly
chart.
- An hourly break of continuity in price action during the New York lunch hour, you can see that here.
- We drop back down into the possible efficiency, hits the order block here. Beautiful rally, accumulates at
the low end of that old hourly imbalance (the pink shaded area) in here. There you go.
- Rallies once more, and you're going to want to go through your charts and see what these levels are.
- This is the part, this is the part where the lazy students, the casual readers, the 'Netflix ICT student,'
you're not going to get this.
- You might be entertained or may be bored to death by watching my videos, but I can tell you this: if you
don't go into your charts and look for the things I'm annotating here on mine, if you're just simply watching
mine, you are not learning. You're not doing the work that's required.
- This is the part that none of my students that paid me for mentorship, they didn't do these things. They
didn't then take that information into live data and watch price print.
- And if they had jobs or if they went to school or if they couldn't be awake during that time, they didn't
make an investment to record price in a screen recorder and then study it that way.
- So, there's no excuse, zero excuse for you not to be going into your charts, because if you don't do that,
you're not going to get the blue ribbon results you're hoping for at the end.
ES 1
